content.text: Most production APIs require authentication. In an agentic tool chain the handler — not the model — is responsible for managing credentials: fetching tokens, caching them, refreshing on expiry, and never leaking secrets into the context window. OAuth 2.0 is the dominant pattern for delegated access to third-party services. Understanding how to implement it correctly inside a tool handler is essential for building agents that access real-world APIs.

attrs.alt: OAuth 2.0 token flow in an agentic tool: handler checks cache, fetches from token endpoint, calls protected API, handles 401 by refreshing

content.text: The model must never see credentials. API keys, client secrets, and access tokens belong in environment variables or a secret store — the handler fetches and uses them transparently.
The simplest pattern: load the key once at startup from the environment and inject it as a header on every request. Never hardcode keys or accept them as tool inputs from the model.
, not the raw response body which might echo back part of the key in some error formats.

Machine-to-machine APIs (Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Workspace service accounts) use the Client Credentials grant. The handler exchanges a client ID and secret for a short-lived access token, caches it until near-expiry, then refreshes automatically.
The 30-second expiry buffer prevents the edge case where a token is valid when fetched but expires before the API call completes.

When the agent acts on behalf of a specific user (e.g. reading their Google Calendar), the Authorization Code flow is required. The user completes a browser-based consent step before the agent session starts, and the resulting refresh token is stored securely for the handler to use.
tells the model (and the application layer) exactly what to do: present the OAuth consent URL to the user before retrying.
Rotate regularly; never accept from model input
Cache token; refresh 30 s before expiry
Acting on behalf of a user (Google, GitHub)
Store refresh token securely; handle re-auth flow
Private key must be stored in vault, not env

import pytest, os
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

def test_api_key_injected_not_exposed(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("WEATHER_API_KEY", "test-key-xyz")
    # Re-import to pick up the patched env var
    import importlib, __main__
    importlib.reload(__main__)

    mock_resp = MagicMock()
    mock_resp.status_code = 200
    mock_resp.json.return_value = {"temp_c": 18.5, "condition": "sunny"}

    with patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_resp) as mock_get:
        result = get_weather("London")

    # Key used in header, never in result
    call_headers = mock_get.call_args.kwargs["headers"]
    assert call_headers["X-API-Key"] == "test-key-xyz"
    assert "test-key-xyz" not in str(result)

def test_expired_token_triggers_refresh():
    _TOKEN_CACHE["expires_at"] = 0.0   # force expiry
    _TOKEN_CACHE["access_token"] = "old-token"

    token_resp = MagicMock(status_code=200)
    token_resp.json.return_value = {"access_token": "new-token", "expires_in": 3600}
    api_resp = MagicMock(status_code=200)
    api_resp.json.return_value = {"data": "ok"}

    with patch("requests.post", return_value=token_resp), \
         patch("requests.get", return_value=api_resp):
        with patch.dict(os.environ, {
            "OAUTH_TOKEN_URL": "https://auth.example.com/token",
            "OAUTH_CLIENT_ID": "cid", "OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET": "csec",
            "API_BASE_URL": "https://api.example.com",
        }):
            result = call_protected_api("/data")

    assert result == {"data": "ok"}
    assert _TOKEN_CACHE["access_token"] == "new-token"

def test_401_returns_auth_error():
    mock_resp = MagicMock()
    mock_resp.status_code = 401

    with patch("requests.get", return_value=mock_resp):
        result = get_weather("London")

    assert result["error"] == "AUTH_ERROR"
    assert "key" not in result.get("message", "").lower()  # no credential detail leaked
The handler — not the model — manages all credentials. Load from environment variables or a secret store; never accept secrets as tool inputs.
For OAuth Client Credentials: cache the access token with a 30-second expiry buffer; on a 401, clear the cache and retry once before returning AUTH_ERROR.

For Authorization Code (user delegation): store the refresh token securely per user; handle the re-auth case by returning AUTH_REQUIRED with an action field so the application layer knows to redirect the user.
Return structured auth errors (AUTH_ERROR, AUTH_REQUIRED) — never echo credential values or raw 401 response bodies.
Test that credentials are injected into request headers but are absent from the returned result dict.
